title: The Seven Sages of Rome (Northern Version)
summary: The checked-in CME00033 XML represents the University of Michigan Corpus of Middle English Prose and Verse item keyed from Killis Campbell's 1907 edition The Seven Sages of Rome. Campbell edited the complete 4,328-line Northern Text C from British Library Cotton MS Galba E IX, ff. 25v–48v, and supplied variants from the incomplete Northern Text R in Bodleian Library MS Rawl. poet. 175, ff. 109r–131v. He also compared Weber's 1810 composite text, whose central fragment comes from the parallel Southern/Y witness National Library of Scotland Adv.MS.19.2.1, ff. 85r–99v, while Cotton supplies its opening and ending. Complete public scans of Campbell and Weber and the complete 701-image public Auchinleck project inventory are cached; the latter is only partial coverage of the physical manuscript. No publicly obtainable complete facsimile of Cotton Galba E IX or Rawl. poet. 175 was verified on 2026-07-12, so exact mediated whole-object imaging routes and reuse distinctions are recorded.
review_notes: The intellectual subject is the anonymous Northern redaction, not the full Seven Sages tradition, the broader Middle English Version A or Y Group, either complete manuscript miscellany, Campbell's apparatus, Weber's composite text, or either electronic representation.

Campbell concludes that C and R independently descend from a lost common exemplar cr; neither witness is modeled as copied from the other, and reconstructed cr and y are not modeled as extant entities.

The XML's 4,328 L elements comprise 4,326 base-text lines and two R-only lines nested in a footnote. Campbell/Cotton base lines 1832 and 3533 are absent, so the matching total is accidental rather than proof of completeness.

entities.label: General Corpus source XML for CME00033
notes: The file is 532,552 bytes and is pinned through the recorded CME submodule commit and blob.
TEMPHEAD records review on 2016-07-25 by lattaj and MURP; it does not establish the initial encoding date.

entities.label: University of Michigan CME digital text CME00033

date_statements.label: documented XML review
date_statements.notes: The revision entry documents review rather than initial keying.

notes: The encoding includes Campbell's title page, edited Middle English text on printed pp. 1–145, a NOTES divider, and the abbreviations list, but excludes the full introduction, substantive notes, glossary, and index.
It has 4,326 base-text L elements in 31 direct work LG elements plus two Rawlinson-only L elements nested in an apparatus NOTE; two Campbell/Cotton base lines are absent.

entities.label: Killis Campbell's 1907 edition of The Seven Sages of Rome

bibliographic.citation: Killis Campbell, ed., The Seven Sages of Rome (Boston, New York, Chicago, and London: Ginn & Company, 1907), Albion Series.
bibliographic.title: The Seven Sages of Rome

notes: Campbell selects C as base because R is incomplete and C is more archaic in spelling and more accurate in minor respects, despite C's later hand.

C is followed except for about twenty stated scribal-error emendations. Campbell supplies punctuation, paragraphing, normalized capitalization, abbreviation expansion, modern word division, lineation, and bracketed manuscript folio and story labels; emendations are bracketed and manuscript readings footnoted.
R variants are generally printed diplomatically except that abbreviations are expanded.

entities.label: Internet Archive scan of Campbell's The Seven Sages of Rome
notes: The complete provider-exposed edition scan is cached; underlying edition rights and scan-file reuse terms are recorded separately.

entities.label: British Library Cotton MS Galba E IX

date_statements.label: production
date_statements.value: first quarter of the fifteenth century (1400–1425)

holding.notes: The catalog record covers ff. 3–114 and locates The Seuyn Sages of Rome at ff. 25v–48v. A separately described Latin Psalter leaf, f. 2, has record 041-001607568; any whole-bound-object request should account for both current descriptions.

physical_description.extent: Current principal catalog item ff. 3–114, plus separately cataloged f. 2; The Seven Sages at ff. 25v–48v
physical_description.format: codex; work copied in two columns
notes: Campbell describes the complete work as 4,328 lines in double columns of forty-seven lines.
The catalog's Digitised Content field was blank when checked.
This is Campbell's complete base witness. Its date and physical features remain witness-layer facts rather than the composition date of the Northern redaction.

entities.label: Bodleian Library MS Rawl. poet. 175

date_statements.label: production
date_statements.value: second half of the fourteenth century (1350–1400)

holding.notes: The official record describes the work only within the codex's broad religious and moral poems; current specialist scholarship locates it at ff. 109r–131v. Richard Rawlinson bequeathed the manuscript in 1755.

physical_description.extent: 136 folios; The Seven Sages at ff. 109r–131v
physical_description.format: codex; work copied in two columns
notes: Campbell describes 3,974 surviving work lines in columns of forty-four lines.
Two leaves after f. 125 are lost, removing the equivalent of C lines 3673–4022; two leaves after f. 123 were misbound after f. 125 and numbered 126–127.
R adds a couplet after C line 2364 and omits two C couplets.
R is the earlier but incomplete independent witness used throughout Campbell's apparatus.

entities.label: Henry Weber's Metrical Romances, volume III

bibliographic.citation: Henry Weber, ed., Metrical Romances of the Thirteenth, Fourteenth, and Fifteenth Centuries: Published from Ancient Manuscripts, with an Introduction, Notes, and a Glossary, vol. III (Edinburgh: George Ramsay and Co. for Archibald Constable and Co.; London: John Murray and Constable, Hunter, Park, and Hunter, 1810).
bibliographic.title: Metrical Romances of the Thirteenth, Fourteenth, and Fifteenth Centuries
bibliographic.publisher: George Ramsay and Co. for Archibald Constable and Co.; John Murray; Constable, Hunter, Park, and Hunter
bibliographic.pages: The Proces of the Seuyn Sages, pp. 1–153

notes: Weber's Proces is not a unitary witness transcription: Auchinleck supplies the central fragment, with Cotton used for the opening and ending. Campbell cites the composite text as W.
Campbell maps the Cotton-supplied portions to C lines 1–134 and 3108–4328; Weber's own lineation and statement describe the transition through the Auchinleck fragment.

National Library of Scotland Adv.MS.19.2.1 (Auchinleck Manuscript)

holding.notes: The Proces of the Seuyn Sages survives fragmentarily at ff. 85r–99v; detached fragments of the manuscript are held in Edinburgh, London, and St Andrews.

physical_description.extent: preliminary leaves i–viii, 334 folios, final leaves ix–xiv, modern endleaves, and detached fragments
notes: The manuscript is associated with London production.
This is a parallel Southern/Y witness used by Weber, not a direct witness of the Northern redaction.

rights.basis: The item page states that the materials are in the public domain and links the DPLA No Copyright–United States rights statement.
notes: The provider's U.S.-specific assertion does not independently dedicate XML markup, interface assets, or third-party reproductions worldwide.

rights.component: 1907 editorial text, introduction, notes, glossary, and typographical arrangement
rights.basis: The work was published in the United States in 1907, before 1 January 1931, the current public-domain threshold stated by U.S. Copyright Office Circular 15A.

rights.component: Campbell's editorial writing and the edition's typographical arrangement
rights.basis: Campbell died in 1937; current UK guidance gives written works a usual term of life plus seventy years and published-edition layout twenty-five years from publication, both elapsed.

rights.component: Internet Archive scan file and interface assets
rights.basis: The PDF is publicly downloadable and the underlying edition is public domain, but no exact item-level open license for the digital scan file as such was independently verified.
notes: Local research caching and public download do not alone establish redistribution permission for the scan file.

rights.component: 1810 editorial text, introduction, notes, glossary, and typographical arrangement
rights.basis: The edition was published in 1810 and Henry Weber died in 1816; both the U.S. publication-based maximum and UK life-plus-seventy and layout terms have elapsed.

rights.component: Internet Archive scan file and interface assets
rights.basis: The PDF is publicly downloadable and the underlying edition is public domain, but no exact item-level open license for the digital scan file as such was independently verified.
British Library digitisation distinguishes images for reference from images for reproduction and applies separate licensing and terms to reuse.
notes: The medieval textual content and ownership of newly supplied photographs are separate questions; written reuse permission should be retained with any files.

rights.component: prospective Bodleian-supplied reference scans or commissioned manuscript photographs
rights.basis: Bodleian separates mediated reference copying from Imaging Services and indicates that supplied files remain subject to service terms and separate publication or reuse arrangements.

rights.component: Auchinleck project images, transcriptions, descriptions, essays, and reference material
rights.basis: The project's exact terms state that its texts, essays, images, descriptions, and reference material are copyrighted, permit personal use, and prohibit unauthorized commercial use or publication.
notes: The ignored local bundle is a private research cache and must not be staged or redistributed.

NLS's current general PSI policy may allow reuse of many faithful digitization images, but applicability to this legacy project and its contrary exact terms is unresolved; the more restrictive project-specific statement controls until clarified in writing.

editorial_practices.source_feature: Two independent Northern witnesses survive, but R is incomplete.
editorial_practices.representation: C is the continuous base text and R is represented through apparatus variants.
editorial_practices.description: Campbell chose the complete Cotton Text C as base because R is incomplete and because he judged C's spelling more archaic and its minor readings more accurate despite its later hand.
editorial_practices.scope: Edited Middle English text on pp. 1–145

editorial_practices.source_feature: R preserves an independent but incomplete descendant of lost cr; Weber's W is a composite comparison text.
editorial_practices.representation: R and W variants appear in footnotes to the C-based text.
editorial_practices.description: Campbell reports R variants throughout its surviving extent and cites readings from Weber as W, keeping both comparison sources subordinate to the C-based edited text.
editorial_practices.scope: Text and footnote apparatus on pp. 1–145

editorial_practices.source_feature: Manuscript abbreviations in C and R
editorial_practices.representation: Expansions are supplied; expanded letters or words in the edited text are italicized.
editorial_practices.description: Campbell expands abbreviations in both the base text and R variants, marking expansions typographically in the edited text rather than reproducing abbreviation signs diplomatically.
editorial_practices.scope: Edited text and apparatus on pp. 1–145

editorial_practices.representation: Capitalization is normalized in the printed edition.
editorial_practices.description: Campbell explicitly normalizes capitalization as part of the reading text.
editorial_practices.scope: Edited Middle English text on pp. 1–145

editorial_practices.source_feature: Sparse or manuscript-specific punctuation and word division
editorial_practices.representation: Editorial punctuation and paragraphing are supplied and word division is modernized.
editorial_practices.description: The readable printed syntax and paragraph structure are Campbell's editorial presentation rather than a diplomatic record of C's punctuation and spacing.
editorial_practices.scope: Edited Middle English text on pp. 1–145

editorial_practices.source_feature: Campbell's edition includes a title page, extensive introduction, edited text, substantive notes, glossary, index, and abbreviations.
editorial_practices.representation: The XML retains the title page, edited text pp. 1–145, a NOTES divider, and abbreviations list.
editorial_practices.description: The U-M encoding selects the complete intended reading-text portion and some apparatus framing, while omitting the full introduction, substantive notes, glossary, and index. Two base-text line omissions remain unexplained and are not assumed to be intentional selection.

evidence.citation: Computational structural and milestone audit of CME00033.xml against Campbell's printed lineation.
evidence.locator: all LG and L ancestors; NOTE-nested apparatus at Campbell line 2364; milestone transitions after Campbell lines 1832 and 3533
evidence.summary: The XML has 32 LG and 4,328 L in total, but one NOTE-nested LG contains two R-only variant lines. The 31 direct work LG contain only 4,326 L, omitting Campbell/Cotton lines 1832 and 3533. Increasing milestone offsets confirm the two omissions.

open_questions.question: Can a complete color facsimile of the entire current bound British Library Cotton MS Galba E IX be obtained and retained locally?
next_steps: Ask British Library Reference Services whether complete existing photography, legacy microfilm, or an unindexed internal surrogate exists.

If commissioning photography, request binding exteriors, boards or covers, spine, pastedowns, endleaves, blanks, separately cataloged f. 2, all of ff. 3–114, adjacent works, and edge views if offered—not only ff. 25v–48v.
Map the work separately to ff. 25v–48v with exact image locators and first/end deep links.
Confirm private retention and publication or redistribution permission separately in writing before caching supplied files.
notes: No Cotton manuscript image was cached because no public complete-object deliverable was verified.

open_questions.question: Can a complete color facsimile of Bodleian Library MS Rawl. poet. 175 be obtained and retained locally?
next_steps: Ask Bodleian whether complete existing photography, microfilm, or an unindexed internal surrogate exists.
If ordering images, request binding exteriors, boards or covers, spine, pastedowns, endleaves, blanks, all 136 folios, adjacent works, and edge views if offered—not only ff. 109r–131v.
Map the work separately to ff. 109r–131v and document the lost and misbound leaves.
Confirm retention and publication or redistribution terms separately before caching supplied files.
notes: No Rawlinson manuscript image was cached because no public complete-object deliverable was verified.

open_questions.question: Why are Campbell/Cotton base-text lines 1832 and 3533 absent while two Rawlinson-only lines are represented as L elements inside a footnote?
next_steps: Perform a full automated and visual collation of the XML against Campbell pp. 1–145.
Ask U-M whether these are known keying defects or inherited source-package features.
Do not use count(//L) as a base-text completeness test without excluding apparatus descendants.

open_questions.question: What initial keying method, source package, correction history, and source-file reuse terms apply to the U-M CME00033 encoding?
next_steps: Request item-level production metadata and any downloadable source package from U-M.
Keep the 2016 TEMPHEAD entry scoped to review rather than initial creation.
Compare U-M display, repository XML, and Campbell before labeling differences intentional.

open_questions.question: Does the National Library of Scotland's current general PSI reuse policy supersede or modify the legacy Auchinleck project's project-specific personal-use and no-publication terms?
next_steps: Ask NLS for a written ruling specific to the 701 Auchinleck project images and any locally retained bundle.
Keep the bundle private and unstaged until the conflict is resolved.
Record any supplied license separately from permission to retain private research copies.
